planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in moist to wet, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun in spring; globeflower requires consistently moist soil and struggles in dry or hot conditions.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun, spacing 12–18 inches apart; globeflower is a late-spring to early-summer perennial with nearly perfect spherical, clear-yellow to orange flowers on upright stems above deeply divided, dark-green foliage — imagine a large, wild-looking double buttercup. It thrives beside ponds, streams, and in bog gardens where the soil stays reliably moist, and performs particularly well in cool northern climates where summers are mild. In hotter zones (7+) it struggles and often declines by midsummer. Divide every 3–4 years in late summer after the foliage dies back. Modern hybrids of T. x cultorum offer orange, lemon-yellow, and cream flower colors and are more vigorous than the straight species.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions in early fall or container plants in early spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade; leopard's bane is one of the earliest-blooming spring perennials and goes dormant by midsummer — mark its location.

directions: Plant divisions in early fall or set out container plants in early spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; leopard's bane is among the first perennials to bloom in spring, producing bright yellow daisy-like flowers in March or April above heart-shaped, hairy basal leaves — a welcome sight when virtually nothing else is in color. The plants go completely summer-dormant by July; mark their locations so they are not disturbed during the dormancy period. Combine with hostas and ferns that will fill in the gaps left by dormant leopard's bane by summer. Divide congested clumps every 2–3 years in late summer just before the new fall rosettes emerge. Excellent for cutting.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant rooted stem divisions or container plants in moist to wet, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun in spring; creeping jenny spreads rapidly by stolons and is ideal for stabilizing stream banks, edging water features, or filling in gaps between stepping stones.

directions: Plant stem divisions in moist to wet soil in partial shade to full sun where a rapidly spreading groundcover is desired; creeping jenny forms a dense, mat-forming carpet of round, bright-green (or chartreuse-yellow in the 'Aurea' form) coin-shaped leaves studded with small yellow flowers in late spring. It roots wherever stems touch moist soil and spreads quickly to cover bare ground, suppress weeds, and soften the edges of water features and containers. Excellent for shade gardens, pond banks, and as a trailing container plant that cascades over the edges. Can be invasive in naturalized areas near waterways in some regions — check local guidelines. Trim with shears in midsummer if it outgrows its allotted space.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions in moist, humus-rich, well-drained to moist soil in partial shade to full sun in spring; gooseneck loosestrife spreads aggressively by underground rhizomes and should be contained or planted where unrestricted spread is acceptable.

directions: Plant divisions in moist, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; gooseneck loosestrife takes its name from the gracefully curving, S-shaped white flower spikes that nod and arch like a goose's neck in midsummer, creating a distinctive and charming visual effect for 4–6 weeks. The plant spreads vigorously by creeping rhizomes to form dense colonies that are highly effective as a large-scale groundcover but can overwhelm smaller plants — site it where space is generous or install buried barriers to limit spread. Fall foliage turns attractive yellow-orange and provides late-season color. Plants perform well in moist meadows, rain gardens, and the edges of naturalistic plantings. Divide every 2–3 years in spring to reinvigorate and contain.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in moist to wet, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun in spring; meadowsweet thrives in boggy soils, alongside streams, and in rain garden edges where soil stays consistently moist.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun beside ponds, streams, and in boggy areas; meadowsweet is a graceful European native with pinnate, fernlike leaves and large, frothy plumes of tiny, intensely sweet-scented cream-white flowers in midsummer that once gave salicylic acid (the precursor to aspirin) its name. The plant forms large, bold clumps over time and is excellent in naturalistic plantings along water features where it associates well with irises, hostas, and trollius. The golden-leaved form 'Aurea' is striking even without flowers. Do not allow plants to dry out in summer — they collapse quickly under drought stress but recover after watering. Divide every 4 years in early spring.

planting.note: Direct sow in well-drained, poor to average, dry soil in full sun 4–6 weeks before last frost; cupid's dart is short-lived in heavy or moist soils but self-sows to perpetuate itself in sandy, dry, well-drained conditions.

directions: Direct sow in dry, well-drained, poor soil in full sun; cupid's dart produces lavender-blue, papery, cornflower-like flowers on long wiry stems from early summer through fall, borne above clumps of long, narrow, grayish-green leaves. The semi-papery flowers hold their color beautifully when dried and were historically used in love potions (hence the common name). Plants are short-lived perennials in heavy, wet soils but persist for years in lean, sandy conditions and self-sow reliably. An excellent, low-maintenance border plant for dry, sunny sites that pair beautifully with ornamental grasses, eryngium, and phlomis. Divide in early spring or collect seed annually in humid climates where plants are short-lived.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade in spring; bugbane grows slowly but becomes very long-lived once established — choose the permanent site with care.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; bugbane (formerly Cimicifuga) is one of the finest tall shade perennials, producing elegant, towering wands of tiny, fragrant white to cream flowers in late summer and fall, rising 4–7 feet above bold, deeply divided compound leaves. Dark-leaved selections like 'Brunette' (chocolate-purple foliage) and 'Hillside Black Beauty' provide dramatic contrast against lighter companions throughout the season. Plants are extremely long-lived and resent transplanting once established — grow in the same spot for decades. The subtle, sweet fragrance of the flowers carries on the evening air. Divide very carefully in early spring only when absolutely necessary.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in moist, well-drained, moderately fertile soil in partial shade to full sun in spring; mountain fleece is remarkably adaptable and performs well across a wide range of soil and light conditions.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, moderately fertile soil in partial shade to full sun; mountain fleece is a large, trouble-free, long-blooming perennial from the Himalayas that produces slender, poker-like spikes of tiny flowers — typically red, pink, or white depending on the cultivar — from early summer through hard frost, giving it one of the longest bloom seasons of any border perennial at 3–4 months. Bold, large, lance-shaped leaves provide substantial textural presence throughout the season. 'Firetail' (bright red) and 'Alba' (white) are widely available cultivars. Plants are vigorous and adaptable, tolerating clay soil, temporary waterlogging, and moderate drought. Divide every 4–5 years in spring to maintain vigor.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database
